questions.question: Why did 2024+ SOTA text-to-image models (SD3, FLUX, Z-Image) replace the U-Net denoiser with a Diffusion Transformer (DiT)?
options: Transformers are easier to write than U-Nets
DiT has fewer parameters, making training cheaper
U-Net is no longer supported on modern GPUs

DiT scales more predictably (same regime as LLMs), handles long-range spatial dependencies better for prompt-accurate generation, and integrates naturally with transformer text encoders via joint or cross-attention
questions.explanation: DiT treats diffusion as a sequence-modelling task. It scales like language models, avoids the fixed convolutional inductive bias that can bottleneck long-range structure, and integrates cleanly with transformer text encoders. Every 2026 SOTA model — SD3, FLUX.1, FLUX.2, SD4, Z-Image, Qwen-Image — is a DiT. U-Net DDPM is now primarily pedagogical.

questions.question: Rectified flow defines the forward interpolation as `x_t = (1 - t) * x_0 + t * epsilon` (t in [0,1]). What target does the model learn to predict, and why?
options: The clean image x_0 directly, so sampling is a single step
The noise epsilon, identical to DDPM

The velocity v = epsilon - x_0, which is dx_t/dt — the direction of the straight-line path from data to noise; integrating this backward produces clean samples and the near-straight ODE needs far fewer steps
The timestep t itself, used to schedule the noise level
questions.explanation: The velocity target v = epsilon - x_0 is the derivative of x_t with respect to t. Predicting v means the model estimates the direction along the straight-line interpolation. During sampling, an Euler integrator steps backward: x = x - dt * v(x, t). Because the learned ODE is approximately straight (unlike DDPM's curved SDE), 20 Euler steps produce samples that required 1000 steps in DDPM.

questions.question: In `rectified_flow_train_step`, `target_velocity = epsilon - x0` and `pred_velocity = model(x_t, t)`. How does this training objective differ from a DDPM noise-prediction step?
options: DDPM also predicts epsilon - x0; they are the same objective under different names

DDPM predicts the noise epsilon using a cosine beta schedule over discrete steps, while rectified flow predicts the velocity epsilon - x0 along a linear interpolation — a simpler loss on a straighter ODE trajectory
Rectified flow predicts x0 directly; DDPM predicts the velocity
DDPM does not use MSE; it uses a KL divergence loss instead
questions.explanation: DDPM defines x_t by adding schedule noise and trains the model to predict that noise epsilon. Rectified flow defines x_t as a linear interpolation and the target is the velocity v = epsilon - x_0. Both use MSE loss. The difference is the trajectory: DDPM's reverse is a curved SDE requiring many small steps; rectified flow's near-straight ODE allows Euler integration in 20 steps with comparable quality.

questions.question: MMDiT (SD3) uses separate weight streams for text and image tokens that share one joint attention layer. Which architecture described in this lesson extends that design with alternating double-stream then single-stream blocks for efficiency?
options: FLUX (Black Forest Labs 2024) — first N blocks keep separate text and image streams (double-stream, like MMDiT), then later blocks concatenate and share weights (single-stream), reducing parameters at depth while retaining per-modality encoding early
DiT (Peebles & Xie 2023) — plain ViT-style with AdaLN conditioning only
Z-Image — a fully single-stream design at 6B parameters
AdaLN-Zero — the zero-init conditioning module, not an architecture variant
questions.explanation: FLUX's insight is that deep processing is cheaper with shared weights. The first N blocks maintain separate text and image streams (richer per-modality encoding, like MMDiT) while later blocks concatenate the streams and use shared weights for efficiency at higher depth. This double-then-single pattern gives FLUX its characteristic architecture and distinguishes it from both plain DiT and MMDiT.

questions.question: What does AdaLN-Zero mean in a DiT block, and why does the zero initialisation help training?
options: A LayerNorm with epsilon set to zero, preventing division by zero
A LayerNorm applied only to 50% of tokens during training

Adaptive LayerNorm whose modulation MLP is initialised to zero so the block starts as an identity mapping; training then gradually learns the scale, shift, and gate, which stabilises very deep diffusion transformers at the start of training
A LayerNorm with zero mean forcing — equivalent to centering the activations
questions.explanation: AdaLN predicts scale, shift, and gate from the conditioning vector (timestep + text embedding). 'Zero' means the MLP is initialised to all zeros, so gate starts at 0 and the entire block acts as identity. Gradients slowly move the modulation away from zero, preventing the deep stack from diverging early in training. It is the DiT-equivalent of ResNet's residual zero-init and is used in every modern diffusion transformer.

questions.question: FLUX.1-schnell produces an image in 4 steps. Which technique enables this, and how does it relate to FLUX.1-dev?
options: Schnell uses a different base architecture than dev — fewer transformer blocks
Schnell uses smaller image patches, reducing token count
Schnell runs in 4-bit precision; dev runs in bfloat16

Distillation — schnell is trained from the 12B FLUX.1-dev teacher using adversarial/consistency-style objectives so that 1-4 denoising steps match the teacher's 20-30 step output; same architecture and parameter count as dev
questions.explanation: Schnell / Turbo / LCM variants use step distillation. A student (same architecture as the teacher) learns to match the slow many-step teacher's output in 1-4 steps. FLUX.1-dev and FLUX.1-schnell are both 12B parameters with identical architecture — distillation is what makes sub-1-second inference practical. This same idea underlies SDXL Turbo, SD Turbo, and Latent Consistency Models.
